summary: Drew DeVault examines the legal implications of GitHub Copilot being trained on GPL-licensed code, raising the question of whether machine learning outputs constitute derivative works. He highlights that Copilot can reproduce verbatim GPL'd code like Quake III's fast inverse square root, creating a potential licensing conflict. He makes the provocative observation that if ML outputs are derivative works, then GitHub itself—where 300 employees used Copilot daily—may be a derivative work of AGPL software, potentially requiring GitHub to open-source its entire codebase. He broadens the discussion to fundamental ethical questions about consent, rights over training data, and the tech community's unresolved divide over machine learning ethics.
key_points: Copilot can reproduce verbatim GPL'd code, raising the question of whether ML model outputs are derivative works under copyleft licenses
If ML outputs are derivative works, GitHub's own codebase may be subject to AGPL requirements since 300 employees used Copilot in their daily work
The controversy reveals a deeper, unresolved divide in the tech community about the ethics of using others' work as ML training data
The same logic that could 'wash the GPL off' code could also apply to personal information collected without consent
DeVault predicts capitalism will sweep ML ethics under the rug whenever addressing them threatens business models
money_quotes: GitHub Copilot can be used as a means of washing the GPL off of code you want to use without obeying its license.
This may entitle all GitHub users to receive a copy of GitHub's source code under the terms of the AGPL, effectively forcing GitHub to become an open source project.
I'm normally against GPL enforcement by means of pulling the rug out from underneath someone who made an honest mistake, but in this case it would certainly be a fascinating case of comeuppance.
What rights should be, and are, afforded to the owners of the content which is incorporated into training data for machine learning?

I predict that GitHub Copilot will mark one of several inciting events which start to rub some of the glamour off of machine learning technology and gets us thinking about the ethical questions it presents.

I also predict that capitalism will do that thing it normally does and sweep all of the ethics under the rug in any scenario in which addressing the problem would call their line of business into doubt.
key_insight: If machine learning outputs are legally derivative works of their training data, then GitHub's own use of Copilot may have inadvertently made its entire proprietary codebase subject to the copyleft licenses it trained on.
